did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper explicitly states that 'AI-assisted computerized image analysis' was employed to quantify lesion size and pigmentation percentage in dermoscopic images of basal cell carcinoma. The discussion further specifies the use of 'Python and OpenCV-based algorithms' for this quantitative assessment.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled 'TYPE Original Research' and describes a retrospective observational study involving 41 patients with histopathologically confirmed basal cell carcinoma. It presents original data collection, AI-assisted image analysis methods, statistical results, and new findings regarding the correlation of dermoscopic and histopathological features, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Correlation of dermoscopic and histopathological features in basal cell carcinoma using computerized image analysis
brief_summary: This retrospective study analyzed the correlation between dermoscopic and histopathological features in 41 patients with histopathologically confirmed basal cell carcinoma (BCC) from two Turkish hospitals. The researchers employed AI-assisted computerized image analysis using Python and OpenCV algorithms to quantitatively assess lesion size and pigmentation percentage from high-resolution dermoscopic images. Key dermoscopic findings included arborizing telangiectasias and blue-gray ovoid nests as predominant features, while ulceration was strongly associated with infiltrative BCC subtypes. The study investigated the relationship between pigmentation patterns and tumor depth, observing a negative correlation where deeper tumors exhibited reduced pigmentation, although this trend was not statistically significant. The authors concluded that comprehensive characterization of these features, combined with AI-assisted quantitative analysis, enhances lesion differentiation and diagnostic precision for BCC.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study focused exclusively on 42 histopathologically confirmed BCC lesions from 41 patients. Subtypes analyzed included nodular, superficial, mixed, infiltrative, micronodular, and adenoid BCC.

Mentioned in the discussion as a differential diagnosis to distinguish from BCC based on pigmentation patterns (atypical networks/blue-whitish veils vs. melanophages), but not included in the study cohort.
Mentioned in the context of differentiating BCC from other skin malignancies via histopathology, but not included in the study cohort.
Referenced in citation 15 regarding dermoscopy-based radiomics to distinguish BCC, but not part of the current study's data or analysis.

all_task_types.target: Quantitative lesion parameters (lesion size in mm² and pigmentation percentage)
all_task_types.details: The study employed AI-assisted computerized image analysis (Python and OpenCV) to quantify continuous numeric outcomes: lesion size (converted from pixels to square millimeters) and pigmentation coverage ratio (pigmented area divided by total lesion area). The paper explicitly states this approach 'combined expert-driven manual annotation with AI-assisted quantitative analysis' and did not perform automated classification or segmentation as the primary AI task, but rather used AI for measurement of features derived from manual annotations.

datasets_used.name: Nizip State Hospital and 25 Aralik State Hospital Cohort
datasets_used.description: A retrospective cohort of patients with histopathologically confirmed basal cell carcinoma (BCC) evaluated at two Turkish hospitals (Nizip State Hospital and 25 Aralik State Hospital) between April 2023 and February 2025. The dataset consists of high-resolution dermoscopic images acquired using a DermLite DL5 device interfaced with an iPhone 15 Pro Max, alongside corresponding histopathological findings and clinical data retrieved from hospital records.

use.other: The dataset was used for retrospective analysis to correlate dermoscopic features with histopathological subtypes and tumor depth. AI-assisted computerized image analysis (Python and OpenCV) was employed to quantify lesion size and pigmentation percentage from the dermoscopic images.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To compare categorical variables and examine associations between tumor size categories, histopathological subtypes, and dermoscopic features, particularly due to small sample sizes in multiple subcategories.
To analyze linear associations between numerical variables, specifically assessing the correlation between dermoscopic pigmentation percentage and tumor depth.
To analyze nonparametric relationships between numerical variables, specifically assessing the correlation between dermoscopic pigmentation percentage and tumor depth.
To define the confidence level for statistical analyses.
To define the threshold for statistical significance.

hypothesis_testing.test: Fisher-Freeman-Halton exact test, Pearson’s correlation coefficient (r), Spearman’s rank correlation coefficient (ρ)
hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no association between categorical variables (tumor size categories, histopathological subtypes, and dermoscopic features) and no linear/non-linear correlation between numerical variables (dermoscopic pigmentation percentage and tumor depth).
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a significant association between categorical variables or a significant correlation between numerical variables.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: Statistical significance was defined as p < 0.05. The study found that while a negative correlation was observed between pigmentation percentage and tumor depth, this trend was not statistically significant. Associations between tumor size categories, histopathological subtypes, and dermoscopic features were examined using the Fisher-Freeman-Halton exact test.

quantitative_pigmentation_and_depth_corr.method: AI-assisted computerized image analysis (Python and OpenCV)

correlation_statistics.interpretation: Negative correlation observed; deeper tumors exhibited reduced pigmentation

tumor_size_categories.trend_observed: Pigmentation decreased as tumor size increased; transition from pigmented to vascular/ulcerative features with increasing size

Nizip State Hospital and 25 Aralik State.notes: The study found that while a negative correlation was observed between pigmentation percentage and tumor depth, this trend was not statistically significant. Maple leaf-like structures showed a statistically significant correlation with mixed BCC (p = 0.013). Ulceration and multiple erosions were strongly associated with infiltrative BCC.

architecture_used.main_model: Python and OpenCV-based algorithms (computerized image analysis)

techniques: Pixel-to-millimeter conversion ratio calculation based on embedded millimeter scale
Contour detection using cv2.findContours() for lesion margins
Color-based thresholding using OpenCV's inRange() function for pigmented areas
architecture_details.approach_type: Hybrid expert-driven manual annotation combined with AI-assisted quantitative analysis

classification_or_output_head.formula: Pigmentation Coverage Ratio = Pigmented Area (mm²) / Total Lesion Area (mm²)

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Python and OpenCV-based algorithms (computerized image analysis)
preprocessing_techniques: Manual delineation of lesion boundaries and annotation of pigmented structures by a dermatologist using Procreate on an iPad
Standardization of macroscopic and dermoscopic images in terms of lighting and distance
Standardization of dermoscopic images in terms of lighting, distance, and resolution before processing
Calculation of pixel-to-millimeter conversion ratio based on the embedded millimeter scale in the image
Lesion contour extraction using OpenCV's cv2.findContours() function on manually annotated regions
Color-based thresholding using OpenCV's inRange() function to isolate pigmented areas from non-pigmented background
Conversion of pixel-based areas to square millimeters using the established pixel-to-millimeter conversion ratio
Exclusion of the horny layer of the epidermis from total lesion area measurement
conclusions: Comprehensive characterization of dermoscopic and histopathological features of basal cell carcinoma enhances lesion differentiation.
AI-assisted computerized image analysis for lesion size and pigmentation, combined with histopathological evaluation, improves diagnostic precision.
Ulceration and multiple erosions are strongly associated with infiltrative basal cell carcinoma subtypes.
Blue-gray ovoid nests and arborizing telangiectasias are predominant dermoscopic features, while blue-gray dots and globules are most common in micronodular basal cell carcinoma.
A negative correlation was observed between pigmentation percentage and tumor depth, with deeper tumors exhibiting reduced pigmentation, although this trend was not statistically significant.
Increased tumor size correlates with prominent invasive features such as ulceration, hemorrhage, and stromal changes, while pigmentation decreases.
Further studies with larger patient cohorts are needed to validate these findings and refine classification criteria.
important_remarks: The study has a small sample size (41 patients, 42 lesions), which limits the generalizability of the findings and statistical power.
The retrospective design introduces potential selection bias, as inclusion depended on available excisional biopsy specimens and dermoscopic images rather than random patient selection.

The observed negative correlation between pigmentation percentage and tumor depth was not statistically significant (Spearman’s ρ = −0.612, p = 0.144), indicating weak evidence for this specific association in this cohort.
Tumor depth measurements may be influenced by interobserver variability, potentially affecting consistency.
The accuracy of the AI-assisted quantitative analysis depends on algorithm quality, image resolution, lighting conditions, and dermoscopy equipment calibration, introducing possible measurement bias.
The study population is from a low-latitude region with intense UV exposure and Fitzpatrick skin types II–IV, limiting external validity to other demographic or geographic populations.
Using excisional biopsies may lead to classification bias, particularly in mixed histological patterns where certain subtypes could be underrepresented.
The study lacks external validation; the authors explicitly state that further studies with larger, multicenter cohorts are needed to validate findings and refine classification criteria.
